coredata.dc:title: Gelatin-based carbon quantum dot-molecularly imprinted polymer: Safe photoluminescent core-shell nano-carrier for the pH-responsive anticancer drug delivery

Javanbakht, Siamak

coredata.dc:description: This study aims to synthesize a core-shell gelatin-based carbon quantum dot-molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP@g-CQD) via the precipitation free-radical polymerization process using methotrexate (MTX) as a model anticancer template. To investigate the efficiency of the prepared photoluminescent MIP@g-CQD as a pH-responsive nano-carrier, MTX was loaded into MIP@g-CQD by soaking in a drug solution and the release behavior of the loaded drug was evaluated in the necessary pH values (7.4, 5). The successful synthesis of materials was characterized using PL, TEM, FE-SEM, DLS, and FT-IR analyses. Interestingly, the created cavities in the core-shell nano-carriers can interact with the MTX molecules effectively, leading to an increase in the loading capacity. According to the obtained results from Langmuir adsorption isotherms, the imprinting factor was calculated (IF = 4.91). Also, the binding kinetics of MTX revealed the creation of particular recognition sites in the core-shell polymeric network. The MTX-loaded MIP@g-CQD displayed a low rate and limited release at the simulated physiological environment (pH 7.4, 37 °C), but it is increased at tumor tissue (pH 5, 41 °C) conditions, which can lead to long-term and sustained release of MTX in the desired target. This property of MIP@g-CQD could avoid the release of MTX in normal physiological conditions, decreasing the possible side effects of MTX drug. Owing to the existence of amide functional groups in the nano-carrier structure and its negatively charged nature, the MTT assay displayed desirable cytotoxicity against the breast cancer cell line (MCF-7) for the MTX-loaded nano-carrier. According to the obtained results, the prepared safe photoluminescent MIP@g-CQD with appropriate pH-responsivity has a high ability to be applied as an anticancer and bio-detection agent.

Stability of synthesized nano-carrier in PBS buffer solution (the photo on the right was taken 7 h after the one on the left) (a), particle size distributions of g-CQD (b) MIP@g-CQD (c), and NIP@g-CQD (d), zeta potential measurements for MIP@g-CQD (e) and NIP@g-CQD (f).

Static (a) and dynamic (b) adsorption diagrams. Langmuir (c) and Freundlich (d) isotherm diagrams, pseudo-first-order (e) and pseudo-second-order (f) kinetic model curves for MTX loading into the MIP@g-CQD and NIP@g-CQD.

The preparation process of core-shell MIP@g-CQD for MTX-delivery (nano-carrier before rebinding (b MIP@g-CQD), nano-carrier after rebinding (a MIP@g-CQD).

head.ce:title: Gelatin-based carbon quantum dot-molecularly imprinted polymer: Safe photoluminescent core-shell nano-carrier for the pH-responsive anticancer drug delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic Chemistry, Shahid Beheshti University, Daneshjou Boulevard, Tehran, 1983969411, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, Shahid Beheshti University, G. C., P. O. Box 1983963113, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Stem Cell Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Stem Cell Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study aims to synthesize a core-shell gelatin-based carbon quantum dot-molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP@g-CQD) via the precipitation free-radical polymerization process using methotrexate (MTX) as a model anticancer template. To investigate the efficiency of the prepared photoluminescent MIP@g-CQD as a pH-responsive nano-carrier, MTX was loaded into MIP@g-CQD by soaking in a drug solution and the release behavior of the loaded drug was evaluated in the necessary pH values (7.4, 5). The successful synthesis of materials was characterized using PL, TEM, FE-SEM, DLS, and FT-IR analyses. Interestingly, the created cavities in the core-shell nano-carriers can interact with the MTX molecules effectively, leading to an increase in the loading capacity. According to the obtained results from Langmuir adsorption isotherms, the imprinting factor was calculated (IF = 4.91). Also, the binding kinetics of MTX revealed the creation of particular recognition sites in the core-shell polymeric network. The MTX-loaded MIP@g-CQD displayed a low rate and limited release at the simulated physiological environment (pH 7.4, 37 °C), but it is increased at tumor tissue (pH 5, 41 °C) conditions, which can lead to long-term and sustained release of MTX in the desired target. This property of MIP@g-CQD could avoid the release of MTX in normal physiological conditions, decreasing the possible side effects of MTX drug. Owing to the existence of amide functional groups in the nano-carrier structure and its negatively charged nature, the MTT assay displayed desirable cytotoxicity against the breast cancer cell line (MCF-7) for the MTX-loaded nano-carrier. According to the obtained results, the prepared safe photoluminescent MIP@g-CQD with appropriate pH-responsivity has a high ability to be applied as an anticancer and bio-detection agent.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, breast cancer as a prevalent disease is a global concern in the women's health sector [1]. This issue has motivated scientists to conduct further research on effective therapeutic techniques. However, conventional chemotherapy treatments have limitations because they do not provide selective drug delivery leading to exposing healthy cells to therapeutic anticancer agents, followed by various undesirable side effects such as toxicity and multiple drug resistance [2]. One of the best solutions to overcome these issues is the development of nano-carriers. Today, nanomaterials as versatile platforms indicate prominent potential in bioscience and biotechnology such as bioimaging [3–5], biosensing [6], gene/drug delivery [7,8], and photothermal therapy [9], etc. On the other hand, carriers based on nanomaterials are an appropriate platform to participate in biomedical purposes that lead to synchronic and synergistic diagnosis and therapy, facilitate therapeutic performance, and efficiently deliver imaging agents [10,11]. They have triggered innovative approaches in drug delivery and therapeutic by using their exceptional functions in drug transport across biological barriers and intracellular delivery [12–14].

In recent decades, the design and construction of nanoparticles based on molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) as synthetic receptors [15] and their biomedical applications have particularly attracted growing attention in pharmaceutical fields owing to their unique features [16–18]. Molecular imprinting has been processed using a templating technique for generating recognition sites in the polymeric networks with shape-memorized cavities and high affinity to a specific template molecule [19]. MIPs have made continuous and significant progress in various fields, for instance, separation [20,21], extraction [22], sensors [23], catalysis [24], and drug delivery systems (DDSs) [25,26], owing to their excellent selectivity, good physical and chemical stability, inexpensive to produce, and easy preparation [27]. One of the main challenges in DDSs is to design suitable vehicles that can both effectively deliver and sustained release of the desired drug [28]. Interestingly, MIPs as a potential smart platform in DDSs provide the rate-limiting mechanism in controlled release vehicles [29,30]. The function of MIPs leads to sustained release because of the suitable interactions between the template (drug) and the functional monomer in the generated cavities [31]. Moreover, they can keep the drug concentration with a narrow treatment window lower than toxic levels and at the same time above the minimum effective level in the plasma [32].

MIPs possess a lot of advantages but suffer from some significant drawbacks, such as slow mass transfer, irregular shape, difficult template removal, and low binding capacity [33]. However, core-shell imprinting is a highly effective method that could overcome these shortcomings. In the core-shell technique, the binding sites are located near or on the surface MIP layer, allowing for rapid and easy rebinding and removing of the target molecule within the polymeric matrix and finally decreasing mass transfer resistance [34]. Several studies have reported successful use of the core-shell MIP in extraction [35], sensing applications [36], and DDSs [37].

Highly sensitive fluorescence signals originated from different sources, for example, semiconductor quantum dots [38], silicon nanoparticles [39], and various fluorescent dyes [40], which were broadly used in biological applications. However, most fluorescent bio-probes were constructed from semiconductor quantum dots or fluorescent dyes so their toxicity or long-term monitoring may be challenged. Recently, biological interesting carbon quantum dot (CQD) nanoparticles with low toxicity and stable fluorescent signals have gained rising attention [41]. CQDs are novel biological fluorescent probes made from natural carbon sources with a particle size of less than 10 nm [42]. Interestingly, these talented materials have exceptional features, such as excellent excitation, delocalized electrons in a broad surface region, suitable biocompatibility, and solubility in diverse media, chemical inertness, and π-π interactions by diverse bioactive molecules [43–45]. Eventually, CQDs were extensively applied in a variety of biomedical fields, including carriers for drug delivery [46,47], bio-imaging [48], bio-sensing [49], and disease diagnosis [50] due to their low toxicity and good biocompatibility [51].

In the field of CQDs synthesis, Hsu and Chang discovered that materials containing amino and carboxyl functional groups are useful in synthesizing CQDs with high photoluminescent properties [52]. Inspired by their findings, Li et al. suggested that gelatin, abundant in amino and carboxyl functional groups, is an appropriate raw material for synthesizing CQDs [53]. They developed an eco-friendly approach for CQD production by using the hydrothermal treatment of gelatin with pure water, without a complicated post-treatment procedure or the assistance of any chemicals. Gelatin is a relatively water-soluble and low-cost natural polymer obtained from animal skin and bones and has long been employed in film-making, the food industry, and photography. In this regard, Ghosh et al. reported the preparation of CQDs using gelatin as a precursor (g-CQDs) [48]. They examined the cell imaging ability of the prepared g-CQDs by cells regarding to various clinical backgrounds such as bacterial cells, yeast cells, and cancer cell lines. They concluded that the g-CQDs illuminate the cells and can be applied as talent cell labeling non-toxic biomarkers. Accordingly, gelatin-based carbon quantum dots (g-CQDs) are a promising option for stable and long-term fluorescent imaging of diverse categories of cells.

In the present work, we synthesized the photoluminescent core-shell nano-carrier based on functionalized g-CQDs, as bio-based core, coated with MIP via an effective drug imprinting strategy (MIP@g-CQD). Methotrexate (MTX) as an effective anticancer drug prescribed in chemotherapy with life-threatening side effects was used for the imprinting to produce bio-based nano-carrier with selective recognition sites. The novelty of this work is the vinyl-functionalization of the gelatin quantum dots through a simple epoxy ring-opening reaction to covalently participate in the free-radical polymerization reaction as the bio-based core in the structure of the MIP, which leads to the stable core-shell structure for pH-sensitive anticancer drug delivery system [54–60]. On the other hand, the designed nano-carrier was synthesized by utilizing 1,4-bis(acryloyl)piperazine (BAP) as a new cross-linker agent and acrylamide as a functional monomer, and its efficiency for controlled drug release of MTX was investigated for the first time as far as we know. We hypothesize that the produced pH-responsive MIP@g-CQD can potentially control the delivery of the drug that is appropriate for an efficient sustainable drug release in the intended aim. Moreover, designed polymer with brilliant photoluminescent properties is talented to be applied in further fields of imprinted polymers for instance bio-detection, sensors, and selective extraction.

Gelatin, allyl 2,3-epoxypropyl ether, 1,4-bis(acryloyl)piperazine (BAP), acrylamide, ammonium persulfate (APS, (NH4)2S2O8), sodium acetate (NaOAc), potassium hydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and biological reagents were achieved from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Breast cancer cell line (MCF-7) was purchased from the Iranian Blood Transfusion Institute. Methotrexate (MTX) was purchased from Exir Pharmaceutical (Iran). Acetic acid (HOAc), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethanol (EtOH), and methanol (MeOH) were utilized in analytical grade without more purification.

The Raman spectra were achieved by Raman Takram P50C0R10. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) spectra were recorded by STOE diffractometer with Fe-Kα radiation and the thermogravimetric documents (TGA) were obtained by TA. Q600 equipment (USA). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were attained by an FT–IR spectrometer equipped with Bruker Instruments, model Aquinox 55, Germany. The ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectrum of the samples and the photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, model 1700, Japan) and spectrofluorometric (PerkinElmer, LS45, Germany), respectively. The zeta potential measurements and the dynamic light scattering (DLS) were done by DLS-ZP/Particle Sizer (Horiba Jobin Jyovin model SZ-100z, Japan). 1H NMR spectra were achieved by Avance spectrometer (Bruker DRX-30072, 300.13 MHz, deuterated dimethyl sulfoxide, DMSO‑d 6). The field emission scanning microscope (FE-SEM) observations were obtained with an electron microscope (FE-SEM TESCAN MIRA 3-XMU). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were achieved using an electron microscope (Zeiss EM900).

According to the previous report [53], g-CQDs were synthesized via the hydrothermal treatment of gelatin. In a general experiment, 1 g of gelatin was dissolved gradually in 50 mL distilled water at 60 °C for 30 min under stirring. Then, the mixture was added into a 100 mL stainless steel autoclave with a Teflon liner and heated at 200 °C for 3 h to produce light yellow solution. Afterward, the reactor was automatically cooled to ambient temperature, and the obtained light yellow solution was centrifuged for 20 min (10,000 rpm) to eliminate the precipitate and agglomerated particles. Subsequently, a light brown aqueous solution of g-CQDs was dried to light brown powder by freeze-drying process.

To prepare vinyl-functionalized g-CQD [61], 0.1 g of synthesized g-CQDs were added to the flask covered with aluminum foil containing 4 mL ethanol under stirring. Then, 118 μL allyl 2,3-epoxypropyl ether (1 mmol) was added to the resulting solution and the reaction mixture was stirred on a magnetic stirrer at 60 °C for 24 h. After completion of the reaction, the resulting mixture was centrifuged with ethanol and acetone for 10 min (10,000 rpm) and the obtained precipitates were dried in an oven at 60 °C for 3 h.

MIP@g-CQD nano-carriers were synthesized according to the previous reports [62,63] with a little change by precipitation free-radical polymerization using vinyl-functionalized g-CQD as a photoluminescent core, MTX as a template, APS as an initiator, acrylamide as a functional monomer, and BAP as a crosslinking agent. The typical synthetic process was as follows: Firstly, 50 mg prepared vinyl-functionalized g-CQDs were dissolved in 5 mL deionized water under stirring. On the other hand, 23 mg MTX (0.05 mmol) and 29 mg acrylamide (0.40 mmol) were added to a round bottom flask containing 10 mL deionized water and sonicated for 30 min to create the interactions between the template (MTX) and functional monomer. Then, the prepared solution of vinyl-functionalized g-CQDs was added to the flask gradually under stirring. Afterward, 311 mg BAP (1.60 mmol) and 16 mg APS (0.07 mmol) were added to the reaction vessel. Next, the content of the reaction vessel was degassed by ultrasound for 30 min and then purged with Argon gas for 15 min. The polymerization was carried out at 65 °C under stirring and distillation conditions for 24 h. After completion of the reaction, the precipitates were centrifuged for 10 min (10,000 rpm), washed with ethanol, and dried at 50 °C. The MTX molecules were extracted via “Soxhlet apparatus” using a mixture of MeOH/HOAc (9:1 (v/v), 100 mL) as acidic extraction solvent (pH 5) for 72 h to disruption the hydrogen bonds and remove the MTX molecules in washing step. The amount of MTX was measured in extraction solvent by UV–Vis spectroscopy at 304 nm wavelength. The eliminated MTX from MIP@g-CQD in extraction solvent was almost equal with the added MTX during the synthesis step verifying this claim that the MTX molecules do not remain inside the MIP after washing with the solvent. As a control, gelatin-based carbon quantum dot-non imprinted polymer (NIP@g-CQD) was prepared according to the mentioned protocol for the synthesis of MIP@g-CQD with the difference that its synthesis was performed in the absence of template (MTX), which leads to the synthesizing bulk polymer without selective cavities that has only some surface adsorption due to the functional groups on it.

The static adsorption studies were used to evaluate the binding site and imprinting factor (IF) of the synthesized MIP@g-CQD in distilled water. The Langmuir (Eq. (1)) and Freundlich (Eq. Also, “kF” and “n” are the Freundlich constants, demonstrating the adsorption capacity and the adsorption intensity, respectively.

For this purpose, 100 mg of MIP@g-CQD and NIP@g-CQD were separately immersed in 10 mL of MTX solution (100 to 1000 ppm) and continuously shacked for 24 h in the dark. Subsequently, the mixtures were centrifuged for 10 min (10,000 rpm) and then, the MTX concentration of the supernatant was determined by UV–Vis. ce:section.ce:section-title: Loading of MTX & in-vitro MTX release studies
ce:section.ce:para: To loading of MTX into the nano-carriers, 100 mg of MIP@g-CQD and NIP@g-CQD were immersed in 10 mL of MTX stock solution (1000 ppm). The mixture was continually shaken (150 rpm) in the dark condition and at ambient temperature for 72 h. To determine the loading capacity of prepared materials with MTX drug, the supernatant of the loading mixtures was analyzed via UV–Vis spectroscopy. Finally, the drug-loaded nano-carriers were filtered and washed with deionized water to eliminate unloaded MTX, and then dried at room temperature.

The in-vitro release profile of MTX was determined via the drug-loaded nano-carriers [64]. Generally, 10 mg of MTX-loaded MIP@g-CQD and NIP@g-CQD was situated in a dialysis bag (MWCO 6000 Da) and submerged in 10 mL of sodium acetate buffered (SAB) and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solutions in simulated tumor tissue (pH 5, 41 °C) and physiological environment (pH 7.4, 37 °C) conditions, respectively. At definite time intervals, the supernatant of release media (2 mL) was picked up and exchanged with the identical volume of fresh buffer. The amount of released MTX was deliberated by UV–Vis spectrophotometer. To measure the percent of MTX loading and cumulative MTX release, the calibration curve of the standard MTX at λ = 304 nm was used and the following Eq. (2) and Eq. (3), were employed, respectively:
(7)

where, “Qt” is the drug released at t time, “Q0” is the initial amount of drug, and “k” is the release constant.

The cell cytotoxicity of MTX-loaded MIP@g-CQD and NIP@g-CQD was deliberated via employing an MTT assay. Typically, MCF-7 cells were chosen and seeded on 96-well plates at 10000 cells/well density in 200 μL cell medium and incubated for 48 h. Then, they were treated with MTX and MTX-loaded MIP@g-CQD in a similar concentration of the drug. Besides, to evaluate the biocompatibility of blank nano-carriers (MIP@g-CQDs), the cells were treated with them. The cell cytotoxicity was surveyed by eradicating the culture medium and adding 20 μL MTT solution with 5 mg/mL concentration to the 150 μL fresh culture medium in each well. After 4 h incubation at 37 °C, the culture medium was exchanged with 200 μL DMSO to solubilize the formazan crystals. These steps were also done for NIP@g-CQDs and MTX-loaded NIP@g-CQD to investigate and compare the toxicity. All tests were carried out with three repetitions.

Molecular imprinting involves using a templating technique for generating recognition sites in the polymeric networks with a high affinity to a specific template molecule. For this aim, a functional monomer and crosslinker are arranged around a template during the polymerization process. Then, the template molecule is removed, and selective cavities are formed with the functional group positions that match the template molecule's shape. To synthesizing core-shell MIP based on g-CQDs via the imprinting of the MTX template a suggested schematic is shown in Scheme 1 that approves the predictable experimental. Initially, g-CQDs were achieved from gelatin through the hydrothermal process that was completely investigated in the literature [53]. One of the challenges and limitations of the molecularly imprinted polymers is their washing step, which must be done continuously several times until all template molecules are completely removed. Especially, for the core-shell MIPs, the core must be held covalently and sturdy in the polymeric shell to prevent washing. Therefore, the vinyl-functionalization of the g-CQDs surface is necessary because the vinyl-functionalized quantum dot cores can participate in free-radical polymerization covalently due to their stable conjugation to imprinted polymer without removing the g-CQDs during the washing step. For this aim, the synthesized g-CQDs were functionalized with allyl 2,3-epoxypropyl ether during the epoxy ring opening process to afford vinyl-functionalized g-CQDs. Afterward, MIP@g-CQD was produced by a precipitation free-radical polymerization procedure. The polymerization process is proceeded through the reaction of a template molecule (MTX), functional monomer (acrylamide), crosslinking agent (BAP), the vinyl-functionalized g-CQD as fluorescent core, and an initiator (APS) leading to the core-shell MIP@g-CQD (Scheme 1). Eventually, to create recognition sites related to the template molecule, the MTX was eliminated by the “Soxhlet apparatus” method in a mixture of MeOH/HOAc (9:1 (v/v)) solution. The present protocol is beneficial for biomedical applications such as controlled release and bio-imaging.

Also, the reasonable mechanism including the precipitation free-radical polymerization [65] for the generation of MIP@g-CQD is demonstrated in Scheme 2 . Firstly, APS as initiator is decomposed under heating to generate free-radicals, which attack the double bond of acrylamide (as functional monomer) and a radical intermediate is prepared. Then, the growth of chain is carried out by addition of monomer molecules on the growing macroradical. Finally, the crosslink of the polymer chain by BAP as a crosslinking agent is performed in the presence of the template molecules (MTX) leading to the creation of MIP containing high affinity and specificity porous cavities with the recognition binding sites that could generate high electrostatic interactions with MTX. Actually, the shape-memorized cavities created in the synthesized MIPs have amide (-CONH2) functional groups that can interact with the -COOH and -NH2 groups related to the MTX molecules by the hydrogen bonds that leads to increase in the loading capacity.

To find the kind and percentage of different elements in g-CQDs, elemental mapping and EDS analysis were carried out on g-CQDs powder (Fig. 1a). The results showed the presence of C, N, and O in the g-CQDs sample. The weight and atomic ratios (W%:A%) were obtained 49.43: 54.96, 23.85: 22.74, and 26.72: 22.31 for C, N, and O elements, respectively. The Raman spectrum of g-CQDs shown in Fig. 1b depicts two distinct peaks at 1470 and 1598 cm−1, which are related to the D-band and the G-band of graphitic structure [66]. The D-band is corresponded to vibrations of sp3 carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered graphite, while the G-band corresponds to the vibration of sp2 carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice. As shown in the Raman spectrum the intensity of the G-band is higher than the D-band revealing that the majority of the synthesized g-CQDs are principally graphitic in nature [44,67]. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the gelatin and synthesized g-CQDs are exposed in Fig. 1c. The g-CQDs spectrum displays weak broad peaks at around 2θ = 22°. Accordingly, the g-CQDs have larger interlayer spacing (0.41 nm) than that of graphite (0.34 nm) for the C (002) diffraction peak, revealing the presence of numerous functional groups for the synthesized g-CQDs [53,68]. The TGA analysis of g-CQDs and gelatin (Fig. 1d) were performed under air at 25 °C to 800 °C. In the TGA curve of gelatin, the primary weight loss happened below 240 °C that is related to the evaporation of water molecules. The second weight loss in the range of 240–480 °C can be due to the loss of side functional groups in the gelatin chains and the third weight loss at 480–600 °C is attributed to the decomposition of the main chain. In the case of g-CQDs, weight loss at 250–460 °C has happened owing to the loss of the surface functional groups. The residual weights for g-CQDs and gelatin at 800 °C are 20 wt% and 7 wt%, respectively. Noteworthy, more thermal stability of the g-CQDs than the neat gelatin can be owing to the pseudo-graphene structure [44].

To obtain structural insights about the synthetic materials, the FT-IR analysis was considered (Fig. 1S). The FT-IR spectrum of the g-CQDs (a) exhibited two particular absorption peaks representing the stretching vibration of OH (3430 cm−1) and NH (around the range of 3000–3200 cm−1). Moreover, the absorption peaks of CO and CO at about 1700 cm−1 and 1100 cm−1 show the existence of oxygen-containing functional groups in the g-CQDs structure. In addition, the characteristic skeletal vibration in the regions 1450–1650 cm−1 demonstrates the existence of aromatic nucleus. Also, a broad vibration band around 3000–3200 cm−1 and one peak at 1549 cm−1 are characterized by the vibration and deformation bands of NH, exhibiting the presence of amino-containing functional groups [53]. In the vinyl-functionalized g-CQDs FT-IR spectrum (b), the peak growth attributed to the CC band (1549 cm−1), confirms the successful covalent functionalization of g-CQDs by allyl 2,3-epoxypropyl ether [69]. NIP@g-CQD (c) and MIP@g-CQD (d) displayed the absorption peaks of CO amide, CC aromatic rings, CO, -CH, and NH groups in the polymeric structure at 1618, 1440, 1216, 2925, and 3453 cm−1, respectively. The presence of the aromatic ring's structure vibration in the FT-IR spectrum of MIP@g-CQD verifies the existence of g-CQDs in MIP@g-CQD and NIP@g-CQD. The FT-IR spectrum of MTX-loaded MIP@g-CQD (e) and NIP@g-CQD (f) exhibit a shoulder peak at 1452 cm−1 and also a wider peak at 1670 cm−1. The appearance of these alterations in bands can expose the successful loading of MTX in the MIP@g-CQD and NIP@g-CQD networks.

To compare the structure of materials before and after modification and polymerization, the 1H NMR spectra of the synthesized g-CQDs, vinyl-functionalized g-CQDs, MIP@g-CQD, and NIP@g-CQD were prepared as their suitable colloidal dispersion in DMSO‑d 6 (Figs. 2S and 3S) [70]. In Fig. 2S the main specific peaks at δ = 8.13 and δ = 7.24 ppm were demonstrated in the 1H NMR spectrum of the g-CQDs (a) that are related to the existence of the aromatic and amide protons, respectively. In addition, the characteristics resonances regarded primary amine were presented at δ = 2.75 and δ = 3.05 ppm that existed in the gelatin structure. Furthermore, owing to the existence of the -NHCH2- and -CH3 groups, the peaks at δ = 3.70–3.96 and δ = 0.82 ppm were observed [71]. Comparison of the g-CQDs (a) and vinyl-functionalized g-CQDs spectra (b) revealed that the hydrogen peak of the amine functional group at δ = 3.05 ppm was disappeared and new peaks of the vinyl protons (δH around 3.50–5.50 ppm) and the methylene protons of -NH-CH2-CHO-CH2-O (δH around 1.05 and 4.3 ppm) regarding amine nucleophilic attack to allyl 2,3-epoxypropyl ether were appeared after functionalization. Also, the accurate construction of the crosslinked polymer (MIP@g-CQD (a) and NIP@g-CQD (b)) was evidently verified by the recognition of methylene protons of the -N-CH2-CH2-N- groups associated with BAP as crosslinker at δH around 1.24 and 2.09 ppm and the methylene protons of the -CH2-CH-CONH2 groups attributed to the acrylamide as functional monomer were appeared at δH around 3.18 and 4.15 ppm (Fig. 3S).

The morphology of nano-carriers was characterized using FE-SEM and TEM analyses (Fig. 2 ). Accordingly, the morphology of MIP@g-CQD and NIP@g-CQD are only a little difference and the nanoparticles possess irregular and spherical surface morphology that attributed to the grafted polymer. Based on the TEM images, the core-shell structure of MIP@g-CQD and NIP@g-CQD is proven in which a polymer coating layer successfully surrounds the inner photoluminescent core involving g-CQDs. The average particle size for MIP@g-CQD and NIP@g-CQD is determined to be about 90 and 120 nm, respectively. On the other hand, the imprinting method that led to the special recognition sites decreased the size of the prepared nano-carrier. This occurrence is related to high interaction between functional monomer and MTX as template that enhanced the polymeric beads nuclei in the polymer matrix during polymerization process leading to the precipitation of the nanoparticles with the smaller diameter [72].

In a follow-up investigation, dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis were prepared for g-CQD (˂10 nm), MIP@g-CQD and NIP@g-CQD that expose a hydrodynamic size for MIP@g-CQD and NIP@g-CQD (about 300–400 nm) larger than the nanoparticle size measured from the TEM analysis (about 70–100 nm) (Fig. 3 ). Actually, this is owing to the inaccuracy of utilizing the DLS technique in measuring the hydrodynamic size of dark nanomaterials/particles. Furthermore, we cannot ignore the effect of the aggregation of nanoparticles [73]. Additionally, the amount of zeta potentials −28.8 and − 25.1 mV were determined for the prepared MIP@g-CQD and NIP@g-CQD, respectively in distilled water. The negatively charged of the prepared nano-carriers is probably attributed to a large number of amide functional groups in their scaffold. The charged value of the MIP@g-CQD was greater than that of NIP@g-CQD maybe owing to the remained amount of MTX even after eliminating the drug in washing step. Moreover, owing to the strong negative surface charge on synthesized polymer, they indicated a lower tendency to aggregate in water (stable in buffer solution (PBS) without aggregation even after 7 h), so it can lead to better dispersion in blood flow and also show the high affinity of interaction with the cell membrane [74].

To investigate the functional stability of the synthesized nano-carrier, drug loading was investigated over four cycles. For this purpose, a specific amount of nano-carrier was placed in the MTX solution and after the drug loading process, the percentage of drug loading determined with the UV–Vis spectroscopy. Then, the nano-carrier was washed several times to completely release the loaded MTX and loading process was performed again, and this procedure was repeated for four cycles. According to the results, only a negligible loading percentage was reduced in each cycle, which can be considered the possibility of the remaining drug in cavities from the previous cycle. Also, to evaluate the structural and morphological stability of the nano-carrier, FT-IR and FE-SEM analyses were considered from the nano-carrier after four cycles (Fig. 4S). As can be seen in FT-IR spectra, all the important peaks were observed for the recycled nano-carrier [75]. Therefore, according to the constant percentage of drug loading in four cycles, FT-IR, and FE-SEM results, it can be concluded that the core-shell nano-carrier is completely stable and suitable for drug delivery applications.

The luminescent properties of the prepared nanomaterials were investigated using the PL spectroscopy technique. As illustrated in Fig. 4 the maximum emission wavelength of g-CQDs was recognized at 430 nm in the emission spectrum, which is attributed to the ordinary absorption of the n–π* transitions of the carbonyl groups or an aromatic π system [52,76,77]. The fluorescence emission behavior of g-CQDs was investigated in various conditions such as different concentrations, various excitation wavelengths, and necessary pH values (7.4, 5). As shown in Fig. 4a, emission intensity of g-CQDs directly depends on the concentration of g-CQDs and the emission intensity increases with increasing the concentration. Also, the PL spectra indicate the maximum emission intensity transfers to a longer wavelength with decreased intensity when the excitation wavelength enhanced (Fig. 4b). Accordingly, the strongest emission peak shifts from 430 nm to 580 nm and the intensity reduce gradually when the excitation wavelength changes from 300 nm to 500 nm. Moreover, the change in pH values has no effect on the emission intensity (Fig. 4c). Although the high basic fluorescence properties of g-CQDs were induced in the vinyl-functionalized g-CQD, MIP@g-CQD, NIP@g-CQD, MTX-loaded MIP@g-CQD, and MTX-loaded NIP@g-CQD (Fig. 4d), but the PL intensity is lower than that of the neat g-CQDs. Probably, nucleophilic attack of the amino group on allyl 2,3-epoxypropyl ether for vinylation of the g-CQDs surface reduced some lone-pairs participating in resonance, which caused a reduction in the PL intensity of MIP@g-CQD. In addition, the coated g-CQDs by polymerization for the production of MIP@g-CQD and NIP@g-CQD and the loading of MTX on them, are the reasons for reducing their PL intensity. Interestingly, luminescent properties exposed that the synthesized MIP@g-CQD can be utilized as a bio-detection platform. We suggest supplementary research in this attractive field.

To evaluate the adsorption (loading) process, the static and dynamic adsorption data were provided for MIP@g-CQD and NIP@g-CQD (Fig. 5 (a, b)). Fig. 5(c, d) illustrates the linearized form of the Langmuir and Freundlich for synthesized MIP@g-CQD and NIP@g-CQD and the correlation coefficient and other constants of the two isotherm models are exhibited in Table 1 . The results revealed the static adsorption data of NIP@g-CQD fitted with the Langmuir model had a higher correlation coefficient (R2) than the data fitted with the Freundlich model, demonstrating that monolayer adsorption occurs on the functional groups dispersed on the NIP@g-CQD surface through physical interactions. The adsorption behavior of MIP@g-CQD is well fitted with the Freundlich isotherm model. The obtained value of the R2 = 0.9939 provides a good description of the surface adsorption process, and based on the Freundlich isotherm, it can be noted that the adsorption is carried out in a multilayered manner [78]. Firstly, the adsorption process performs in a single layer that includes functional groups on the surface. Subsequently, adsorption occurs in the following layers through the interactions (hydrogen bond) between -CO2H and -NH2 groups present in MTX molecules and -CONH2 groups present in the cavities of MIP until saturation is performed. According to the obtained results from the Freundlich isotherm, the cavities in the synthesized core-shell nano-carriers created by molecular imprinting significantly increase interactions with the MTX molecules leading to high loading capacity. These isotherm models are the best fit for our experimental studies. Also, the IF = 4.91 was achieved from the Langmuir adsorption isotherm, which reveals the successful imprinting of the synthesized polymer.

Moreover, a kinetic model study (Fig. 5(e, f)) revealed that the curve fitted to a pseudo-second-order kinetic model had a better R2 value than a pseudo-first-order kinetic model for MIP@g-CQD, representing that the adsorption of MTX onto MIP@g-CQD proceeded via chemical adsorption. The high electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonds between the amide groups of MIP@g-CQD and acid and amino groups of MTX all strengthen the chemisorption of MTX by MIP@g-CQD [32]. Owing to the lower physical interactions between the NIP@g-CQD and MTX, the R2 value of the curve fitted to a pseudo-first-order kinetic model was higher than that of a pseudo-second-order kinetic model (Table 2 ).

The loading capacity of MTX was calculated using UV–Vis spectroscopy to be 41.2 ± 0.5 % and 80.14 ± 0.5 % for NIP@g-CQD and MIP@g-CQD, respectively. A high loading capacity was detected for MIP@g-CQD due to the extra recognition binding sites (3.8. Binding kinetics and isotherms) [79,80]. To clarify the ability of the prepared nanoparticles as a pH-sensitive anticancer nano-carrier, an in-vitro MTX release test was performed. Fig. 6 displays the MTX release profiles of the NIP@g-CQD and MIP@g-CQD in the imitated physiological environment (pH 7.4, 37 °C) and tumor tissue (pH 5, 41 °C) conditions. The MTX release rate is enhanced with time and it is higher at pH 5 than that of pH 7.4 for MIP@g-CQD. This phenomenon is attributed to an extra interactions of MIP@g-CQD with MTX molecules due to the recognition binding sites that exhibited slow and controlled drug release at pH 7.4. This property of MIP@g-CQD could avoid the release of MTX in normal physiological conditions, decreasing the possible side effects of non-specific MTX drug. On the other hand, owing to the lack of specific recognition sites in the NIP@g-CQD matrix, there are limited interactions between the drug and the nano-carrier and the pH-sensitivity is very low. Also, the effect of temperature was studied on the release process and the results showed that temperature changes (pH 7.4, 41 °C & pH 5, 37 °C) have no effect on the drug release behavior of MIP@g-CQD and NIP@g-CQD and pH is only effective factor on drug release. The main mechanisms of release in the MIP@g-CQD that leads to controlling MTX-release are solvent diffusion and polymer chain relaxation, as well as the minor one, is polymer swelling [81].

In continuation of our investigation, the Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas drug release models were employed to evaluate the behavior and mechanism of drug release. Fig. 7 displays an excellent R2 of the fitted drug release diagrams for MIP@g-CQD in pH 5.0 and 7.4 solutions, revealing that the drug release behavior of MIP@g-CQD is consistent with the models and the mechanism is controlled by Fickian diffusion [32,82]. Also, the Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas kinetic models parameters provided in Table 3 .

The cytotoxicity of the synthesized materials was investigated through an MTT assay against MCF-7 cell lines. It indicated a dose-dependent behavior after incubation for 48 h, and high cytotoxicity was obtained via a high-content incubation of active drugs (Fig. 8 ). Owing to the existence of amide functional groups in nano-carrier and its negatively charged nature, MTX-loaded nano-carrier have prominent cytotoxicity against MCF-7 cell [83]. For 48 h of the incubated time, the IC50 values were calculated to be 500 μg/mL for the MTX-loaded MIP@g-CQD. It should be noted that, while the MTX-loaded MIP@g-CQD shows lower cell toxicity than free MTX, it can deliver MTX at a controlled release and reduces its possible adverse effects. Besides, the cell viability (up to 80 %) in the presence of nano-carriers without drugs, reveals their suitable biocompatibility in the body's environment. Results exposed that the synthesized MIP@g-CQD could be a valuable nano-carrier for anticancer DDSs.

In a follow-up investigation, performance comparison between the core-shell MIP in literature and the synthesized MIP@g-CQDs nano-carrier for the anticancer drug release was provided in the Table 4 . The drug release of the synthesized bio-based nano-carrier for MTX as the anticancer drug has been highly effective in terms of release percentage and significant cytotoxicity toward breast cancer cells.

The present work aimed to employ modified g-CQDs, as a bio-based core, for the preparation of MTX-imprinted core-shell nano-carrier for potential pH-sensitive anticancer drug delivery. The synthesized materials were investigated by different analyses, and the achieved results verified the high efficiency of the current strategy for drug release. The binding isotherm and kinetic experiments verified the recognition sites with high affinity and selectivity toward MTX in the MIP@g-CQD structure compared with prepared NIP@g-CQD. In addition to experimental studies, Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm studies for NIP@g-CQDs and MIP@g-CQDs samples, also confirm the maximum adsorption and high efficiency for the MIP@g-CQDs compared to NIP@g-CQDs. Moreover, based on the results obtained from drug control release studies, the low drug leakage at pH 7.4 from MIP@g-CQD can effectively reduce side effects on healthy tissues. Importantly, owing to the significant improvement in drug release at pH 5, the prepared nano-carriers could greatly increase the cytotoxicity for cancer tissues. This phenomenon is attributed to an extra interaction of MIP@g-CQD with MTX molecules due to the recognition binding sites that exhibited great controlled drug release. Additionally, the MTT analysis exposed that MTX-loaded MIP@g-CQD has highly effective cytotoxicity toward MCF-7 cells. The results exhibited that the MIP@g-CQDs nano-carrier could be employed as a safe potential bio-based platform for anticancer DDSs.
